figures.caption: Figure 7: Surrogate predictions versus experimental measurements for observable facility nodes during steep power changes (0→ 10→ 5→ 7 kW). Shaded bands denote 95th-percentile confidence intervals from M = 64 parallel ensemble rollouts.
Figure 4: Horizontal, compact representation of the 3-loop thermal system graph topology.
Figure 3: SAM-based digital twin model of the experimental thermal-fluid facility used for synthetic trajectory generation.
Table 5: No-plot rollout speedup factors for single-model and ensemble inference, computed as simulated (predicted) time divided by wall-clock runtime.

Figure 5: Forecasting comparison across the four transient scenarios listed in Table 3. The red dashed line corresponds to non-perturbed GNN-ODE predictions, while the red background indicates the predictive uncertainty band between the 5th and 95th percentiles. The green background denotes the imposed power profile on the secondary y-axis. GT (SAM) denotes SAM ground truth, which is used to compare against GNN-ODE predictions and compute MAE.

Figure 8: Inferred thermal trajectories for permanently uninstrumented (hidden) nodes during steep power changes (0→ 10→ 5→ 7 kW). Initial states are seeded by TGMI; shaded bands show 95th-percentile confidence intervals from M = 64 ensemble rollouts.
Table 2: Nodal discretization and signal mapping scheme (“Uninstrumented” nodes are latent states with mi = 0).

Figure 1: Architecture of the proposed physics-informed GNN-ODE surrogate for thermal-hydraulic forecasting under partial observability. Plant-node features are represented as [Tnorm,mask, Ṫnorm], and a topology-guided missing-node initializer (TGMI) provides initial estimates for uninstrumented nodes. Type-specific MLP encoders map plant, actuator, and ambient inputs to a shared hidden space H, followed by a three-layer GNN encoder in which plant states are updated via physics-informed message passing while actuator and ambient states are propagated unchanged. A controlled latent Neural ODE, integrated with Runge Kutta 4-th order (RK4), advances the latent state, and the decoder predicts node-wise temperature increments ∆̂T ∈ R|Vp|, where |Vp| is a number of plant nodes. The control input u is assembled from actuator-node and flow-node features.
Table 7: Forecasting rollout summary for missing nodes (virtual sensors + GNN-ODE forecast).
Table 6: Forecasting rollout summary for observed nodes (GNN-ODE forecast).
Table 4: Reconstruction performance for missing nodes on validation dataset.

Figure 2: CAD model of the experimental thermal–hydraulic facility illustrating the three-loop configuration and the locations of key instrumentation and control components. Flow directions are indicated by colored arrows: primary loop (red), secondary loop (yellow), and tertiary loop (blue).
Figure 6: Long-horizon rollout Mean Absolute Error (MAE) versus forecast horizon on held-out SAM test transients for (a) observed nodes and (b) unobserved (missing) nodes.
